content: To the American contributors to this community, I want to congratulate you on your new President. Despite all the talk, I feel that America has proven itself to be strong and healthy and continues to be the land of the free. That is in the face of so much change and uncertainty.
This topic was moved here from #general > Congratulations on the new President by Nathanael Arkor
inauguration
inauguration
Remarkably, today is the 21st day of the 21st year of the 21st century.
Neat
Wow
yesterday, today and the next 8 days are also a palindrome if you write dates the US way: 1/20/21 as 12021

Cool!

But some idiot decided that there was no 0th century, so the 21st century started in 2000 (or worse, actually 2001), and the date is 1/21/2021, not 1/21/2121.
There wasn't always a deep appreciation for 0, to be fair
It's actually sorta cool that the 0th century happened before people knew about 0. And all the negative centuries happened before people knew about negative numbers!
They didn't know they were living in negative years. :upside_down:

Do you think the current calendar system will last into 5 digits? Probably not, right? There will be a new calendar system for each planet we reach, for starters, and then when the interplanetary re-invasion happens, the invaders will probably install a new system again...
Or alternatively, few enough humans will survive the next 200 years that civilisation will reboot again (assuming anyone survives at all).
I think the current calendar system will last until our civilization really collapses, at least here on Earth.
So the big question you're raising is how long that will take.
Foundation vibes around here
I think instead of using ordinal words like '21st' we should just number our centuries. So the years from 2000 to 2099 would be 'century 20'.

Here's a blog article I wrote about what Biden did about climate change yesterday:

  • US environmental policy (part 3), Azimuth, 20 January 2021.
Some of those bullet points inspired an audible sigh of relief out of me. :relieved:
Neat, we're back on track to cut emissions in time for "a couple of decades too late to have any hope", which is a major step up from "never"

John Baez said:

Cool!

But some idiot decided that there was no 0th century, so the 21st century started in 2000 (or worse, actually 2001), and the date is 1/21/2021, not 1/21/2121.

At least if you write it as 1/21/21 it's palindrome if you do not count the slashes :smile:
We'll do whatever it takes to make this day feel special!

Neat, we're back on track to cut emissions in time for "a couple of decades too late to have any hope", which is a major step up from "never".

I assume you're just joking, Jules. It's never "too late to have any hope".

There is a continuum of disasters from bad to very bad to very very bad to very very very bad.... and this continuum only ends at the point where all life on Earth is extinct. So no matter how late it is, it's worth doing all we can to stop global warming.
For example, I'd be much happier knowing that some humans will survive even if civilization collapses, and I'd be much happier knowing that some life survives even if humans go extinct.
(By the way, I doubt humans will go extinct due to human-caused climate change.)

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

Some of those bullet points inspired an audible sigh of relief out of me. :relieved:

Yeah it's weird to here sane things about POTUS now. Everything not completely stupid/evil he does feels like a huge win. I'm glad we're back to this, though it's eerie we're celebrating being 'non-evil'.
Most of human history has consisted of bouts of evil followed by celebrations of non-evil, no?
(And often those celebrations of non-evil get out of hand and become evil.)

What I am is a bit jealous of Americans for apparently having something to look forward to in politics. No matter how bad Trump was, he could be undone by waiting 4 years followed by a massive election and legal fight. Brexit can't be undone, for all practical purposes it's a one way process. Moreover, you apparently have at least one famous politician who's young, where I'm from that's just inconceivable
@Jules Hedges who's the famous and young politician? (I'm not sure what you mean by young!)
Jon Ossoff gained some notoriety for being one of the two winners of the Senate runoff races in the state of Georgia. He will be 34 in a month. Perhaps that's who Jules was referring to?
If Jules knows who Jon Ossoff is, I'll be utterly shocked!
I thought he was talking about Alexandra Ocasio-Cortez, who is 31.

I assume he's not talking about Amanda Gorman, who is 22:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cNFAICB8vxw
Correct
I've never heard of the others
Actually I thought you had to be 21 to stand for election in the UK, but I just looked it up and it was reduced to 18 in 2006, which I didn't know

It turns out there's an entire wikipedia article about youngest members of the House of Commons and related systems: they're called the "baby of the house" https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baby_of_the_House
Cringey
Amanda Gorman leapt into fame on Wednesday with her inauguration day poem... but she's not a politician.

It makes sense that Ossoff would be less known outside the US. Three months ago I wouldn't have even known him from a plumber – his general importance to this point is more that the Senate Majority Leader and the Senate Minority Leader traded roles as a result of his election. Asking Jules which party now has the Senate majority would probably be like asking me which party is the Opposition. I just remember recently reading that Ossoff is young.
Ocasio-Cortez, on the other hand, is properly famous (or infamous, depending on your political leanings). I knew she's young, but I hadn't been reminded of it recently.

'Infamous'? Merriam-Webster defines this as, "(1) having a reputation of the worst kind : notoriously evil; (2) causing or bringing infamy : disgraceful; (3) convicted of an offense bringing infamy". I can't think of anything AOC has done which could possibly merit any of those descriptions.

She's a young, intelligent and articulate Hispanic woman with progressive ideas. Many people find that combination threatening and alarming, including not a few fellow Democrats.

Well, many people leaning on the right consider AOC as a sort of devil incarnate that will try to steal your money with tax in any conceivable way. I clearly do not agree with that, and I second nearly all the things she proposes. Still, I can see how for many people the adjective "infamous" would perfectly describe AOC.
I mean, the narrative of AOC as literally the worst that the left has to offer has been circulated relentlessly by many right-wing media since she set foot into congress.
It sounds like 'infamous' is being repurposed to describe someone whose ideas you (not you, Fabrizio) strenuously disagree with.
One's ideas are part of their reputation, aren't they?

I'll tire of this soon (which perhaps indicates I shouldn't have brought it up in the first place), but normally I think of 'infamy' more as referring to something mired in or tainted by scandal. Off-hand I can't summon a much better explanation of how I understand the word. Whoops, this train is pulling up to the stop where I want to get off.

I would posit (to pick an non-politician example) that Alexandra Elbakyan is famous (to some extent) among researchers, but infamous among publishers. I've even seen people say things like "Alexandra Elbakyan? Who? Oh, that's right, I see her waving at me every day".

I am vaguely aware of some high-profile US politicians that aren't party leaders, but much less aware of UK politicians. I definitely couldn't name anyone remotely 'young' (though there was a young Scottish lady that got a seat in the Commons a few years back, I vaguely recall).

Possibly Australia can outdo both the US and the UK: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wyatt_Roy elected to Parliament at the age of 20, and appointed a Minister at 25 (albeit a non-cabinet position!)
Looking up the UK example, it was https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mhairi_Black (Wyatt Roy was younger, as it turns out, when he was elected)

While I regret the unintentional trigger, I stand by the intended sentiment of my parenthetical comment that someone of a very different political slant would consider Ocasio-Cortez to be infamous (though probably not among those who would congratulate the new President, so I should have considered the name of the thread before including that parenthetical). I am not endorsing of any opinions regarding her here; I merely acknowledge their existence, as I suspect a very broad political spectrum is represented in the people active on this Zulip.

So I would think that calling Trump 'infamous' would definitely be correct, because of the multitude of scandals surrounding him. But would you say that for instance Margaret Thatcher is 'infamous'? As far as I'm aware, she did not have many scandals, yet her politics are despised by quite a large group of people.

I run a Dungeons and Dragons group where one of the players' characters is a dwarf who had their mining town decimated by a fearsome blue dragon by the name of Margadon Thatchwing. Certainly, that dragon is infamous in the fantasy world where this adventure takes place.

On a more serious note, what counts as scandalous surely is also dependent on your political and moral leanings. My straightforward understanding of 'infamous' is "well-known for bad reasons", and amongst people at one end of the political spectrum, it's conceivable that someone at the other end could become infamous in this sense.

John van de Wetering said:

So I would think that calling Trump 'infamous' would definitely be correct, because of the multitude of scandals surrounding him. But would you say that for instance Margaret Thatcher is 'infamous'? As far as I'm aware, she did not have many scandals, yet her politics are despised by quite a large group of people.

Hell yes xD You can ask to everyone living north of Birmingham and to everyone living in Argentina about it xD
(And you can ask me too, actually xD)

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

On a more serious note, what counts as scandalous surely is also dependent on your political and moral leanings. My straightforward understanding of 'infamous' is "well-known for bad reasons", and amongst people at one end of the political spectrum, it's conceivable that someone at the other end could become infamous in this sense.

I as well always parsed "infamous" as "famous for bad stuff". To me, this seems compatible with point 1 of the definition from Merriam-Webster.

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

I run a Dungeons and Dragons group where one of the players' characters is a dwarf who had their mining town decimated by a fearsome blue dragon by the name of Margadon Thatchwing.

If you want to ruin the lives of all the people of all the people in a town, being a dragon is a good way to do it. But if you want to destroy the lives of all the people in an entire country, you'd better become a politician
(every thread about politics involving a critical mass of british people will eventually degenerate into Thatcher-bashing, this is a universal law)
The terminal object of bashings

Jules Hedges said:

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

I run a Dungeons and Dragons group where one of the players' characters is a dwarf who had their mining town decimated by a fearsome blue dragon by the name of Margadon Thatchwing.

If you want to ruin the lives of all the people of all the people in a town, being a dragon is a good way to do it. But if you want to destroy the lives of all the people in an entire country, you'd better become a politician

Oh she is a politician. Destroying mining towns was a big part of her campaign platform :stuck_out_tongue_closed_eyes:
Probably Garret Miller, arrested this week for threatening to assassinate Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez during the insurrection, considered her "infamous". She gets lots of death threats.
